title: It Happened Again
author: Sam Riley
orientation: Sam Riley argues that the Supreme Court's Bostock v. Clayton County ruling is yet another instance of judicial legislation that exposes the bankruptcy of the pro-life movement's court-centered strategy. He calls abolitionists to defy Roe and stop treating the Supreme Court as the final word on law and morality.
audiences: Pro-life conservative wary of court-centered strategy
Constitutional originalist concerned about judicial activism
Skeptic of the abolitionist critique of the GOP
State legislator weighing whether to nullify Roe

questions.question: What recent Supreme Court decision is Riley reacting to and why does he say abolitionists were not surprised?
questions.audience: Pro-life conservative wary of court-centered strategy
questions.answer: Riley is reacting to Bostock v. Clayton County, in which the Court ruled that the 1964 Civil Rights Act's protection against sex discrimination also covers transgenderism and homosexuality. Abolitionists were unsurprised because they have long warned that the Court legislates from the bench, and the ruling simply confirmed that warning.
questions.quote: It was no shock to those who have seen the writing on the wall for some time, and no one who has consistently advocated abolishing abortion in defiance of the court was embarrassed when it happened again.

questions.question: How does Riley characterize the Supreme Court's interpretive method in Bostock?
questions.audience: Constitutional originalist concerned about judicial activism
questions.answer: He calls it 'Constitutional-blank-pageism' - the practice of treating the Constitution and statutes as blank pages onto which the Court can write whatever left-wing policy it wants, redefining ordinary words like 'sex' to import ideologies that were nowhere in view when the law was written.
questions.quote: In *Bostock v. Clayton County*, the supreme court unilaterally altered the meaning of words and read into the law far-left policy objectives which plainly are not there.

questions.question: Why does Riley believe pro-life Republican judicial appointments have failed?
questions.audience: Pro-life conservative wary of court-centered strategy
questions.answer: Because the strategy of electing Republican presidents to appoint pro-life justices to overturn Roe has not worked: 47 years after Roe, two of those very appointees joined a sweeping post-modern decision on a social issue, proving the court-centered approach is bankrupt.
questions.quote: Here we are 47 years after Roe and two of our pro-life Republican supreme court appointments have just given us one of the most post-modern, far-left decisions on a social issue that we've ever seen.

questions.question: What posture does Riley accuse the pro-life establishment of taking toward the federal judiciary?
questions.audience: Skeptic of the abolitionist critique of the GOP
questions.answer: He accuses them of perpetual groveling - of waiting passively for a hostile court to grant permission to protect preborn children, even as that court repeatedly humiliates them. He frames it as lying supinely on their backs and clinging to false hope.
questions.quote: The conservative establishment of our day has resolved themselves to perpetual groveling to a federal judiciary that perpetually stomps on their face.

questions.question: How does Riley use Patrick Henry's words to indict the 'just wait' mentality?
questions.answer: He invokes Patrick Henry's rhetorical questions about gathering strength through inaction to argue that pro-life leaders have functionally answered 'yes' - they have chosen irresolution and delusive hope over effectual resistance, and so remain bound hand and foot.
questions.quote: The rhetorical questions of Patrick Henry have been resoundingly answered in the affirmative, "Shall we gather strength by irresolution and inaction? Shall we acquire the means of effectual resistance by lying supinely on our backs and hugging the delusive phantom of hope, until our enemies shall have bound us hand and foot?"

questions.question: What practical course of action does Riley call abolitionists to?
questions.audience: State legislator weighing whether to nullify Roe
questions.answer: He calls them to disabuse themselves of the Court's supposed ultimate power and to act by defying Roe and abolishing abortion at the state level, refusing to wait for the Court's permission to protect preborn children.
questions.quote: We must disillusion ourselves from the supposedly ultimate power of the court. We must do this by defying *Roe* and abolishing abortion.

questions.question: How does Riley frame obedience to an unjust Supreme Court ruling theologically?
questions.answer: He frames it as moral catastrophe: a court that orders the murder of children must not be obeyed, and a nation that has obeyed Roe for nearly half a century stands under God's judgment and needs God's help.
questions.quote: If the court said we must allow the murder of toddlers, God help the man who obeys, and God help the nation which has obeyed for 47 years.

related.title: Supreme Court Idolatry at Texas Alliance for Life
related.reason: Develops the same indictment of pro-life groups treating the Supreme Court as the ultimate authority on abortion.

related.title: June V. Russo Must Be the Death Knell of Pro-Life Judicial Supremacy and Incrementalism
related.reason: Extends the Bostock-era critique to another 2020 SCOTUS abortion decision and explicitly takes on judicial supremacy.

related.title: Pro-Life Idaho State Rep: "We all know nullification doesn't happen in reality."
related.reason: Documents the legislative version of the 'just wait' mentality Riley attacks - pro-life officials refusing to nullify Roe.

related.title: Five Reasons Abolitionists Oppose Incremental Pro-Life Bills
related.reason: Lays out the strategic case against the court-deferential incrementalism Riley says has failed.

related.title: WATCH: How the Seven-Pronged Pro-Life Political Strategy Has Failed
related.reason: Companion video-form argument that the GOP-and-courts strategy Riley critiques has not delivered after 47 years.

related.title: Nullification
related.reason: Provides the constitutional foundation for the 'defy Roe' action Riley urges abolitionists to take.

related.title: Are Nullification's Advocates the "Villains" of American History?
related.reason: Defends the historical legitimacy of state defiance of unjust federal rulings, the very remedy Riley prescribes.
